talk_title: Spatial single-cell analysis of supratentorial ependymoma subtypes

models.name: 60+ fresh/frozen supratentorial ependymoma tumors
models.genotype_or_key_features: YAP1 fusion, CEFTA/RELA fusions, various subclusters

models.name: neurosphere and laminin cultures
models.genotype_or_key_features: stem cell media, co-culture with astrocytes/neurons

MOA_summary: Single-cell RNA‑seq and 10x Xenium spatial transcriptomics were used to map developmental cell states across supratentorial ependymoma subtypes, revealing that YAP1‑driven tumors are differentiated ependymal‑like, while CEFTA/RELA tumors retain neuroepithelial and embryonic‑like stem states; spatial organization correlates with a mesenchymal‑hypoxia cell state, and in vitro/in vivo models show that differentiation requires microenvironmental cues.
key_findings: Assembled a cohort of >60 supratentorial ependymoma samples covering all molecular subclusters.
YAP1‑fusion tumors project to later developmental stages and consist almost exclusively of differentiated ependymal‑like cells.
CEFTA/RELA‑fusion tumors contain a mixture of neuroepithelial‑like stem cells, embryonic‑like cells, and differentiated ependymal/neuronal states.
Spatial transcriptomics identified two global tumor architectures (organized vs disorganized) driven by the presence of a mesenchymal‑hypoxia cell state.
In vitro cultures grown as PDX or neuron/astrocyte co‑cultures recapitulate the full differentiation hierarchy, whereas neurosphere‑only cultures enrich for stem‑like cells.

In vivo two‑photon imaging showed distinct morphologies and migration patterns: stem‑like cells are slow/proliferative, neuronal‑like cells display saltatory migration, and ependymal‑like cells are stationary with dynamic projections.
open_questions: What molecular mechanisms drive the transition from organized to disorganized spatial architecture over time?
Do specific cell states confer resistance to radiation or future targeted therapies?

talk_title: Targeting ACOD1‑itaconate axis to suppress ZFTA‑RELA fusion in ependymoma

models.name: mouse neuronal stem cells expressing ZFTA/RELA
IOE immune‑intact mouse model of ZFTA/RELA ependymoma

MOA_summary: ZFTA/RELA fusion upregulates ACOD1, leading to increased production of itaconate; itaconate acts as an oncometabolite that inhibits KDM5 histone demethylases, raising H3K4me3 at the fusion promoter and sustaining fusion expression. Pharmacologic or genetic inhibition of ACOD1 (dimethyl itaconate, citraconate analogs) or upstream glutamine metabolism (GHU‑083) reduces itaconate levels, lowers H3K4me3, down‑regulates fusion mRNA and protein, and prolongs survival in mouse models.
key_findings: ZFTA/RELA fusion drives strong up‑regulation of ACOD1 and accumulation of itaconate in both mouse and human ependymoma cells.
shRNA knockdown of the fusion or ACOD1 reduces cell viability and ACOD1 expression.
ACOD1 whole‑body or tumor‑cell specific knockout in mice dramatically improves survival (median age 25 days → >50 % long‑term survivors).
Dimethyl itaconate treatment lowers fusion mRNA and protein levels in vitro and in vivo; rescue with exogenous itaconate restores fusion expression.
Itaconate inhibits KDM5 demethylases (IC50 ~470 µM), increasing H3K4me3; KDM5 inhibition phenocopies itaconate’s effect on fusion expression.
Glutamine antagonist GHU‑083 reduces itaconate production, lowers fusion levels, and extends survival in IOE mouse models.
Pharmacologic ACOD1 inhibition prevents spinal metastasis in treated mice (0/7 vs 7/7 in controls).
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Partial survival benefit in ACOD1 knockout mice suggests residual ACOD1 allele may sustain tumor in ~50 % of animals.

combination_strategies.rationale: Both ACOD1 inhibition and KDM5 inhibition increase H3K4me3, driving fusion downregulation
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic reduction of fusion protein levels in vitro

combination_strategies.rationale: Blocks upstream substrate for itaconate synthesis
combination_strategies.phenotype: Survival benefit comparable to ACOD1 knockout in mouse models

open_questions: Can selective ACOD1 inhibitors with better brain penetration be developed for clinical use?
What is the impact of ACOD1 inhibition on normal immune cells and macrophage function in patients?

talk_title: Anti‑GM‑CSF therapy reverses immunotolerance in pediatric brain tumors

targets.dependency_context: tumor‑derived CSF in mouse models of ependymoma, CPC, medulloblastoma

biomarkers.name: CSF GM‑CSF concentration
biomarkers.finding: Reduced after Lenzilumab treatment in all three tumor models

models.name: preclinical mouse models (ZFTA‑RELA ependymoma, CPC, group 3 medulloblastoma)

clinical_data.population: mouse models of ependymoma, CPC, medulloblastoma

MOA_summary: Tumor‑derived GM‑CSF in the CSF drives expansion of MHC‑II⁺ hematopoietic stem cells in calvarial bone marrow, which differentiate into immunosuppressive myeloid cells and promote T‑reg development; intrathecal or intravenous Lenzilumab blocks GM‑CSF, reducing stem‑cell proliferation, myeloid suppression, and restoring CD8⁺ T‑cell anti‑tumor activity, leading to tumor regression in multiple pediatric brain tumor models.
key_findings: CSF of tumor‑bearing mice contains high GM‑CSF levels, absent in tumor‑free controls.
GM‑CSF promotes expansion of CD34⁺MHC‑II⁺ hematopoietic stem cells in calvarial marrow, which migrate to dura and tumor.
These stem cells differentiate into immunosuppressive myeloid cells; CD4⁺ T cells skew toward FoxP3⁺ Tregs.
Intrathecal Lenzilumab lowers CSF GM‑CSF, reduces skull HSC proliferation, decreases tumor‑infiltrating myeloid cells, and increases CD8⁺ T‑cell infiltration.
Tumor regression observed in ZFTA‑RELA ependymoma, choroid plexus carcinoma, and group 3 medulloblastoma models; effect replicated with intravenous dosing.
Combination of Lenzilumab with checkpoint inhibition prolongs remission beyond monotherapy.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Tumors eventually relapse with CD8⁺ T‑cell exhaustion, suggesting need for checkpoint blockade.

combination_strategies.rationale: Prevent CD8⁺ T‑cell exhaustion after GM‑CSF blockade

open_questions: Will anti‑GM‑CSF therapy be effective in adult glioma patients with similar HSC expansion?
What are the specific antigens presented by the MHC‑II⁺ HSCs in the tumor context?
